title: Fixing Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator with Union Types

statement.markdown: Implement a generic math calculator that takes two numbers and a mathematical operation as input. The calculator should perform the operation and return the result. However, the code contains a subtle bug related to type inference with union types. Fix the bug to ensure the code works correctly for all test cases. The code should work with both integers and floats, and support addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division operations.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be an array of three elements: the first two elements are the numbers, and the third element is the operation (as a string). The numbers can be either integers or floats. The operation can be one of 'add', 'subtract', 'multiply', or 'divide'.
statement.outputFormat: The output will be the result of the mathematical operation.
statement.notes: The code should handle division by zero and return an error message in such cases.

hints.text: Use union types to represent the operation and numbers correctly

editorial.title: Editorial for Fixing Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator with Union Types
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to use union types to represent the operation and numbers correctly. This will allow the code to handle different types of input correctly and fix the type inference bug.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible approach to solving this problem is to use a series of if-else statements to handle each operation and type of input separately. However, this approach can be cumbersome and may not be the most efficient solution.
editorial.optimalSolution: A more optimal solution is to use a dictionary to map the operation to the corresponding function. This will allow the code to handle different operations and types of input more efficiently and elegantly.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness for this solution can be shown by demonstrating that it handles all possible inputs correctly and produces the expected output for each test case.
editorial.dryRun: An example dry run of the code can be performed by testing it with a sample input and verifying that it produces the expected output.
commonMistakes: Not using union types to represent the operation and numbers correctly

functionSignature.python: def calculate(self, numbers: (int | float | str)[] | tuple, operation: str)
